nativeRange.habitat: Rotala Mexicana is a pantropical annual marsh herb found in seasonally inundated wetlands, rice paddies, marsh margins, slow streams, and ephemeral pools across the Americas, Africa, Asia, and Australia — a far wider range than its species name suggests. In the wild it grows in its emersed form (above the waterline), producing bright green, rounded leaves, but it adapts readily to life underwater, where the leaves become needle-thin and may flush green to pink-orange depending on conditions.

decorPreferences: pressurized CO2 injection (25-40 mg/L) for reliable growth
regular dosing of macro and micronutrients including iron
good water circulation to prevent stagnation around fine foliage

summary: Rotala Mexicana is a delicate, small-leaved stem plant found across the tropics worldwide, despite its name. In the aquarium hobby it is grown as a background bush in high-tech planted tanks, where strong light, CO2 injection, and thorough fertilization drive fast growth and green-to-orange-pink coloration.
careNotes:
Rotala Mexicana (Rotala mexicana) is a small-leaved stem plant that has gained a following in the aquascaping hobby, particularly in the 'Araguaia' form collected from the Rio Araguaia in Brazil. Despite its scientific name, the plant is not exclusively Mexican: it is native across the Americas, tropical Africa, South and South-East Asia, Japan, Korea, and northern Australia — a true pantropical species. Several older scientific names remain in circulation, including Rotala pusilla, Rotala pygmaea, and the basionym Ammannia mexicana, though Rotala mexicana is the currently accepted name.

Compared to the more commonly kept Rotala rotundifolia, Rotala Mexicana has noticeably smaller, narrower leaves and a considerably more demanding care profile. It generally requires high-PAR full-spectrum lighting (6500-7000K, 8-10 hours per day), pressurized CO2 injection at 25-40 mg/L, and a balanced macro and micro nutrient dosing regime on a nutrient-rich aquasoil substrate. Carbonate hardness should be kept low, ideally between 0 and 7 dKH, and soft, acidic water in the pH 5-7 range tends to suit it best.

The 'Araguaia' variety is the form most commonly available in trade. It produces fine, pointed, yellowish-green to orange-pink leaves on red-flushed stems, and like other Rotala species, its strongest red colouration tends to develop under bright light combined with low nitrate levels (around 5 ppm) and abundant iron and micronutrients. In the aquarium it is typically placed at the background or, with regular trimming, shaped into a midground hedge in nano and mid-sized aquascapes.

Rotala Mexicana is sensitive to handling — the small leaves bruise easily, and abrupt changes in water parameters can trigger melt. Growers who do not have a high-tech setup with CO2 injection are generally better served by R. rotundifolia, which offers a similar visual effect with considerably more tolerance for lower light and lower CO2 levels.
breedingNotes: Rotala Mexicana is propagated by stem cuttings. Healthy tops 5-10 cm long are trimmed just below a node, the lowest pair of leaves removed, and the cutting replanted directly into the substrate, where new roots and lateral shoots typically develop within a few days under good growing conditions. Pinching the apical bud (the growing tip) on the remaining stem encourages branching, producing a denser, bushier habit over time. The plant can also be grown above the waterline in an emersed state, where its leaves take on a bright-green, rounded form; this approach is sometimes used for nursery propagation or to nurse stressed specimens back to health before re-acclimating them slowly to submerged conditions to avoid melt.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Used for family (Lythraceae), pantropical native range (South America with Central America, Africa, Australia), height (4-12 inches / ~10-30 cm), high lighting, medium-to-high CO2, fast growth, background-with-midground-option placement, nutrient-rich substrate, macro+micro fertilization, propagation by cuttings, and the critical 'thrive or die' care warning (blackening/stunting if any of light, CO2, nutrients is low). Also confirms the Rotala pusilla synonym and 'Mexican Rotala' trade name.

additional.name: Kew Plants of the World Online (POWO)
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy: Rotala mexicana Schltdl. & Cham. (Linnaea 5: 67, 1830), family Lythraceae, order Myrtales. Lists the very broad pantropical native distribution (Mexico, Central America, most of South America, much of tropical Africa, South and South-East Asia including India/China/Japan/Korea, northern Australia). Annual, seasonally dry tropical biome. 23 synonyms including Ammannia mexicana (basionym), Rotala pusilla, Rotala pygmaea.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database (Rotala mexicana 'Araguaia')
additional.notes: Confirms scientific authority (Cham. & Schltdl.), family Lythraceae, tropical South American + West African + Australian + Japanese range, temperature 18-30 °C (optimal 22-28 °C), pH 4.5-7, kH 0-7 dKH, CO2 25-40 mg/L, high light requirement, fast growth, background/midground/nano placement, propagation by cuttings, and rates care difficulty as 'difficult'. Also documents the low-nitrate red-coloration technique and the blackening/stunting failure mode.

additional.name: Aqualand Pets Plus
additional.notes: Cross-source confirmation of moderate-to-high lighting (6500-7000K, 8-10 h/day), moderate-to-high CO2, moderate-to-fast growth, midground/background placement, pH 6.0-7.5, temperature 22-28 °C (72-82 °F), height 10-30 cm, propagation by stem cuttings (2-4 inch cuttings below nodes), and a moderate-to-difficult care rating. Reinforces the importance of nutrient-rich substrate and regular liquid fertilizer dosing.

propagation: head cuttings (5-10 cm, cut below node)
